content:
3 min read
Just now

--

What started as a curiosity about JavaScript engine internals became an experimental runtime and a deep dive into SpiderMonkey’s embedding APIs.
Why build another JavaScript runtime?
Most JavaScript runtimes today are built on V8 or JavaScriptCore.
Instead of following the same path, this project explores Mozilla SpiderMonkey to better understand how JavaScript runtimes work internally.
The goal isn’t to build a replacement for Node.js, Deno, or Bun.
The goal is to learn:

• How JavaScript engines are embedded.

• How native APIs are exposed.

• How callbacks are executed.

• How an event loop works.

• How garbage collection interacts with native code.
The first surprise
One of the first APIs implemented was:
setTimeout(() => {
print("Hello");
}, 1000);At first, it seemed like exposing a native function would be enough.
JS_DefineFunction(
cx,
global,
"setTimeout",
SetTimeout,
2,
0
);It wasn't.
The difficult part wasn’t registering the function.
The difficult part was deciding when the callback should execute.
JavaScript execution is synchronous
SpiderMonkey evaluates a script synchronously.
JS::Evaluate(cx, opts, src, &rval);
Given:
print("start");
setTimeout(() => {
print("timer");
}, 1000);
print("end");
The expected output is:
start
end
timerThat means the callback cannot execute while JS::Evaluate() is still running.
The solution
Instead of executing callbacks immediately, setTimeout() simply schedules work.
When JavaScript calls:
setTimeout(...)the runtime:

• validates the callback

• stores it as a rooted SpiderMonkey object

• calculates the expiration time

• inserts it into a timer queue

• returns immediately
After JS::Evaluate() finishes, the runtime enters its own event loop:
runtime.run();
The event loop continuously checks for expired timers and invokes callbacks through SpiderMonkey:
JS::Call(
cx,
thisValue,
callback,
JS::HandleValueArray::empty(),
&rval
);The timer wasn’t the hard part
The surprising part was that most of the work had nothing to do with timers.
Most of the learning happened while understanding concepts like:

• JSContext

• JS::Value

• JS::RootedObject

• Garbage collection rooting

• Realms

• Native function bindings

• Callback lifetime

Documentation
One challenge throughout the project has been documentation.
Compared to V8, there are relatively fewer embedding-focused resources for SpiderMonkey.
A significant part of the learning process involved reading SpiderMonkey’s C++ headers and source code to understand how the public embedding APIs fit together.
That experience is one of the reasons this project exists. As development continues, the plan is to publish more articles explaining SpiderMonkey embedding in a beginner-friendly way.

Final thoughts
Building a runtime has been less about writing JavaScript and much more about understanding what happens beneath it.
Implementing a seemingly simple API like setTimeout() ended up leading into garbage collection, object rooting, native bindings, callback lifetimes, and event loop design.
That has made the project a rewarding way to learn how JavaScript engines and runtimes are put together.
If you’re interested in SpiderMonkey, runtime architecture, or JavaScript engine internals, feedback and discussion are always welcome.
GitHub:
https://github.com/varadTheDeveloper/forge-runtime
Download :
https://github.com/varadTheDeveloper/forge-runtime/releases/tag/v0.1.0
